The Route

how to find a moving kitchen

We're a brick-and-mortar that happens to move. The only way to catch us is the calendar — markets, pop-ups and private gigs across the Kitsap Peninsula, April through October. Subscribe and we'll never lose you.

A2

The Band

liner notes

Sourdough Social Club is a love letter to the days when the local spot wasn't where the kids did homework — it was where their parents came for a nightcap. Where community mattered, the food spoke louder than the chef, and the supply chain served the ingredients, not the other way around.

The food spoke louder than the chef.

That's why we started with markets. Markets are the community. We're bakers, pâtissiers, chefs and hosts — three restaurants founded in Berlin, time under master German bakers, Michelin kitchens and James Beard teams across four continents. After nearly two decades abroad, we came home to show Washington what we'd been cooking.

The less distance between plate and seed, the better.

JESSICA★ LEAD VOCALS

Live Culture, Bad Attitude

A starter kept alive year-round. She doubles, she domes, she sulks if you're late. Every loaf and every pie on this bill started in her jar.

48hr cold ferment 1:1 water / flour fed @ 50% weight never sleeps
By Hand

Fed, scored, fired over wood. If a machine could do it, we wouldn't bother. The thumbprints are the point.

Slow Or Nothing

Minimum 48-hour cold ferment. We don't rush Jessica and we don't rush you.

Short Chain

Flour from Cairnspring in Burlington. Meat, produce, dairy from people we call by name.

For The Block

BBQ tastes better in a backyard. We're here for the community, not the brand.

B3

B-Sides

the sourdough wiki — no gatekeeping
Starter Care · No. 01

How To Feed Jessica
(Or Your Own)

A starter is a living culture of wild yeast and bacteria. Feed it on a schedule, keep it warm, and it'll hand you predictable, powerful fermentation. Neglect it and it'll let you know — loudly, then quietly, then never.

The Feeding Ratio

Ours is dead simple: feed 50% of the starter's weight, equal parts water and flour (1:1). Consistency beats cleverness.

Example — 100g Starter
  • Discard down to 100g of starter
  • Add 50g room-temp water
  • Add 50g bread flour (or a 50/50 whole-wheat blend)
  • Stir until no dry flour remains
  • Cover loosely, hold at 70–75°F
  • Feed again in 12–24 hrs depending on activity
When She's Ready

Doubled, domed, bubbles all the way through. The float test is a decent shortcut, but peak activity is the truth-teller. Use her at the top of the rise.

Common Mistakes

Water too hot kills the culture. Heavy chlorine drags fermentation — filtered or left-out overnight is safer. And don't fear the hooch (grey liquid on top) — it's just alcohol, a sign she's hungry, not dead. Pour off and feed.
